title: LaTeX Lite
tagline: A JavaFX desktop app that lets you build LaTeX documents without learning LaTeX — structured input, live preview, copy-paste output.

screenshot.alt: LaTeX Lite main interface with live preview

blurb: LaTeX Lite is a JavaFX desktop application that lets users write structured documents — paragraphs, sections, lists, code blocks, file paths — through a visual interface, with a live preview that mirrors what the compiled LaTeX will produce. When the document is ready, the user copies the generated LaTeX into any compiler (MiKTeX, Overleaf) and gets the formatted document back. The point is the on-ramp: the visual layer absorbs every syntactic gotcha a first-time LaTeX user would otherwise have to learn.
pullQuotes: The hardest part of LaTeX isn't LaTeX. It's getting started.
Tools that teach by hiding complexity, not by hiding consequences.
Live preview is the difference between learning a tool and fighting it.

highlights.title: Document configuration
highlights.lede: Pick the document type before you start typing.
highlights.body: The flow opens with a structured form for document type, attributes, and metadata — class, margins, font size, author — backed by JavaFX combo-boxes and validated input. Configuration is editable later from the main interface, so first-time users aren't punished for guessing wrong.

highlights.title: Sections, paragraphs, lists
highlights.body: Sections and paragraphs are added through a sidebar that mirrors LaTeX's document tree. Lists are built through a dedicated dialog with add/reorder/delete controls — and the preview updates after every keystroke. The structure stays valid by construction; the user never sees an `\itemize` block in the wrong place.

highlights.title: Code blocks & file paths
highlights.body: Code blocks and file-path snippets — the two things first-time LaTeX users always escape wrong — get their own dedicated input modes. Special characters are escaped automatically, syntax-aware formatting is applied, and the preview shows exactly what will render.

highlights.title: Generate & export
highlights.body: A single button compiles the visual document into LaTeX source. The output is copy-paste-ready for any LaTeX compiler — no project files, no compilation step, no dependency tree. The preview the user saw in the app matches the document they get back from MiKTeX.

challengesHeadline: Designing for someone who's never written LaTeX.

challenges.title: Mapping LaTeX's tree to a UI that doesn't feel like a tree
challenges.body: LaTeX documents are deeply nested by nature. A literal tree-view would have scared off the audience the tool was built for. The fix was an outline-style sidebar that flattened the structure visually but preserved it in the underlying model — so 'add a sub-section' was a button, not a drag-and-drop into the right node.

challenges.title: Keeping the preview honest
challenges.body: A preview that doesn't match the compiled output is worse than no preview at all. The preview engine and the export engine share a single template layer — what gets rendered in the UI is the same string that gets written to the .tex file, formatting-for-formatting. There's no second source of truth.
